title: Endemic amphibian species
subtitle: [Endemic species](#dod:endemic_species) are those known to occur naturally within one country only.
caption:
Endemic amphibian species, 2025

Endemic species are those known to occur naturally within one country only.

The choropleth map displays the distribution of endemic amphibian species across countries in 2025, utilizing a color scale that ranges from pale yellow for zero endemics to deep blue for counts exceeding 300. The highest concentration of endemic species, represented by the darkest blue shade indicating values above 300, is visible in Brazil, Colombia, and India. Several other nations, including the United States, Mexico, China, Australia, and Madagascar, are shaded in medium-to-dark blue tones, placing them generally within the 30 to 300 range. In South America, countries such as Peru, Ecuador, and Venezuela also exhibit darker blue shading consistent with higher counts, while Argentina and Chile appear in lighter blue bands corresponding to the 10 to 30 range. Across Africa, the distribution is varied; Madagascar stands out in a dark blue band, whereas nations like Tanzania, Cameroon, and South Africa fall into the intermediate teal and light blue bands representing 10 to 100 species. Much of Europe, Russia, and the northern parts of North America are colored in pale yellow or very light green, indicating either no endemic species or counts near the lower boundary of 1 to 3. Large portions of the Sahara Desert, the Arabian Peninsula, and central Asia also display these lowest values. Greenland and several island nations are marked with a hatched pattern, signifying that no data is available for those regions. The visual gradient highlights a distinct clustering of high endemicity in tropical and subtropical regions of the Americas and Asia, contrasted with lower counts in higher latitudes and arid zones.
